documents.title: Oklahoma State University public AI posture sources

ai_strategy.summary: Oklahoma State's AI posture is teaching-and-learning led, with Microsoft Copilot availability, ITLE AI resources, AI Bytes workshops, and an emerging MS in AI computer-engineering track.

key_provisions: Use institutionally reviewed AI tools for sensitive university work.
Protect restricted, confidential, student, personnel, and research data when using AI tools.
Verify AI output and keep humans accountable for academic, administrative, and research decisions.
ai_policy.summary: Oklahoma State University's public AI policy posture is permissive and emphasizes responsible use, privacy, and human accountability based on reviewed sources.

bodies.scope: Oklahoma State's AI posture is teaching-and-learning led, with Microsoft Copilot availability, ITLE AI resources, AI Bytes workshops, and an emerging MS in AI computer-engineering track.

governance_structure.summary: Governance appears distributed across AI-facing offices, centers, technology units, teaching units, or committees; no standalone chief AI officer was confirmed unless named in leadership.

initiatives.significance: This is a concrete R1 signal of AI moving from isolated experimentation toward governed, mission-linked adoption.

ai_academic_programs.name: MS in Artificial Intelligence - Computer Engineering Track

computing_infrastructure.summary: Oklahoma State's Pete supercomputer is available to OSU researchers and has 6,688 processor cores, more than 2 PB of storage, and 10 GPU nodes with at least two NVIDIA Quadro RTX6000 GPUs each, totaling 22 GPUs. OSU also announced an NSF-funded successor intended to expand GPU-powered regional research capacity.

regulatory_context.summary: State/system context was checked for OK. Relevant public AI or system governance links are included where found.

narrative_summary: Oklahoma State University is an R1 institution whose public AI posture is best understood as follows: Oklahoma State's AI posture is teaching-and-learning led, with Microsoft Copilot availability, ITLE AI resources, AI Bytes workshops, and an emerging MS in AI computer-engineering track. The evidence touches governance and policy, teaching and learning, research and innovation, enterprise enablement, student experience, workforce development, and equity or public-impact dimensions. The strongest public sources are first-party AI hubs, guidance pages, research center pages, academic-program materials, and state or system policy resources. Compared with the most centralized AI adopters, several details remain opaque, including named executive AI ownership, exact enterprise user counts, and total AI spending. Even so, the reviewed public record supports a medium-confidence assessment that AI is moving into curriculum, research infrastructure, technology governance, and student or workforce-facing programs rather than remaining purely ad hoc.

gaps_acknowledged: No public chief AI officer was confirmed in reviewed sources.
No total institution-wide AI investment amount was found.
Exact enterprise AI user counts or contract values were usually not public.
Oklahoma State discloses Pete GPU capacity and Copilot availability, but not Copilot user counts, enterprise licensing cost, or the new NSF supercomputer final GPU configuration.

strategic_archetype.rationale: Primary archetype selected from visible public signals: governance led.
